intro:
Ammon, the land of the “sons of Ammon,” lay on the Transjordan plateau east of Gilead, between the Jabbok and the desert, bordering Moab to the south and the Israelite tribes to the west. Like Moab it sprang from Lot: its ancestor was Ben-ammi, born to Lot’s younger daughter after the destruction of Sodom (Genesis 19:38). Its capital was Rabbah (Rabbath-Ammon, the modern Amman), and its national god was Milcom, or Molech, to whom children were offered in fire.

Because of the kinship through Lot, Israel was forbidden to harass Ammon in the wilderness (Deuteronomy 2:19); yet Ammon proved a persistent enemy. The Ammonites oppressed Israel in the days of the judges until Jephthah the Gileadite defeated them (Judges 11:32–33). Nahash the Ammonite besieged Jabesh-gilead and threatened to gouge out every right eye, which roused Saul to his first victory (1 Samuel 11:1–11). When David sent envoys of kindness to Hanun son of Nahash, the king shamed them, provoking the war in which Joab besieged and David took Rabbah (2 Samuel 10:1–4; 2 Samuel 12:26–29).

Ammon’s idolatry snared even Solomon, who built a high place for Milcom (1 Kings 11:5; 1 Kings 11:7), and the worship of Molech with its child sacrifice drew the prophets’ rebuke. For their cruelty, their gloating over Judah’s fall, and their seizing of Israel’s land, Amos, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and Zephaniah pronounced judgment on Ammon and Rabbah (Amos 1:13–15; Jeremiah 49:1–6; Ezekiel 25:1–7; Zephaniah 2:8–11). After the exile Tobiah the Ammonite opposed Nehemiah’s rebuilding of Jerusalem (Nehemiah 4:3).

Yet even Ammon is not beyond the reach of mercy. Jeremiah’s oracle of doom ends with a promise to “restore the fortunes of the Ammonites” (Jeremiah 49:6), a hint that the God who judges the nations also means to gather them. In Christ the dividing wall of hostility is broken down, so that even the old enemies on Israel’s borders may be brought near by his blood (Ephesians 2:13–14).

link_tables.title: Places

cells: Rabbah
Capital of Ammon (Rabbath-Ammon), besieged and taken by David (2 Sam 12:26)
Heshbon
Plateau city on Ammon’s contested western border (Jer 49:3)
The Jabbok
River marking the old border of the sons of Ammon (Num 21:24)
Aroer
Town near Rabbah on the edge of Ammonite territory (Josh 13:25)
Jabesh-gilead
Israelite town Nahash the Ammonite besieged (1 Sam 11:1)
Mizpah of Gilead
Where Jephthah mustered to fight the Ammonites (Judg 11:29)

link_tables.note: Click any name to open its article. Markers for these places appear on the map above.

cells: Lot
Father of Ben-ammi, ancestor of the Ammonites (Gen 19:38)
Jephthah
Judge who subdued the Ammonites for Israel (Judg 11:33)
Nahash
Ammonite king who besieged Jabesh-gilead (1 Sam 11:2)
Hanun
King who shamed David’s envoys and sparked war (2 Sam 10:4)
David
Conquered Ammon and took the crown of Rabbah (2 Sam 12:29–30)
Joab
David’s commander who led the siege of Rabbah (2 Sam 11:1)
Tobiah
Ammonite official who opposed Nehemiah’s rebuilding (Neh 2:19)
Baalis
King of Ammon behind the murder of Gedaliah (Jer 40:14)
